utterances: Good afternoon.
Afternoon.
I think at this point it is the Republicans.
I'm not saying the Democrats are sometimes the gang that can't shoot straight.

But in this case, they have succeeded, and the Tea Party has succeeded, so it's pushing from both sides in creating a - driving a spike in between the -what had been the unassailable base of the Republican Party.
And I think this is going to blow back on them in a very significant way, certainly in the next election cycle in Congress.
Anna, thanks very much for that.
You're welcome.
Yeah, I would only add to that because I think there's a lot of truth in that, that Obama was able to do this by betraying his own base even before the debate started.
You know, a $33 billion cut in the 2011 budget in February was dismissed by Senator Reid as draconian and unworkable.
Instead, he's got Chuck Schumer up there cheerleading for him now.
Exactly.
And so the president moved a long way towards the Republican position, even before the debate started.
And liberals in the Congress, progressives in the Congress, seemed to accept that without really much complaint.
But it put the position - the president in a good negotiating position.
Ken?
Yeah, I was going to say the same thing and basically talking about who gets the blame there.
But it seems like, aside from the Boehner company, it seems like the Tea Party wing of the party is looking for an ideological statement to make and saying: These are our principles.
Unlike Obama and the Democrats, we're not willing to compromise.
We stand up for what we believe in.
Because you look at what Paul Ryan came up with, and he came up with this perhaps politically courageous, but maybe suicidal as well, this budget.
But Obama and the Democrats have not responded in kind.
I mean, what is the Obama plan?
What is the Democratic plan, in addition to the opposite of what the Republicans are proposing?

Yeah, it's difficult for Republicans because, you know, they certainly don't want a shutdown, but they're headed towards a fight that's going to be the most difficult fight, much more difficult than this year if you're talking about Medicare reform and other things.
And, you know, I think - but also a more consequential fight than a few billion dollars just in this kind of spending.
It's about a few trillion dollars.
Right.
Yeah.
It's the difference here.
I mean, Ryan is talking about, you know, over 10 years, several trillion dollars in savings.
The Tea Party is talking about a few billion dollars in the current budget, and they're also stepping all over Ryan's story to some extent - the budget that's moving forward.
So I think that more, kind of, serious establishment Republicans are concerned about that, but you have to remember that many of the Tea Party freshmen, they did not run so much against Democrats.
They ran against the establishment of the Republican Party, and they're looking for the excuse to justify their suspicions, so.

Now, do you find that - if the Republican presidential candidates are going to try to win the affections of the Tea Party and win the primaries because the Tea Party has a lot of sway in the Republican primaries, as we learned last year, are they going to be painted as too far to the right come Election Day?
Well, I think there's been a little movement on that.

Since the announcement of the Ryan budget, most of the intellectual conservative movement, most of the movement conservatives have really rallied around the Ryan budget, and I think a Republican presidential candidate is likely going to have to do that for whatever its risks.
None of them endorsed it, not one single one.
That's true, but I do think it presents, to some extent, the platform for 2012.

They're taking a wait-and-see attitude, but it - I -you know, everyone wanted Republicans who were just focused on domestic discretionary spending for this first period with, you know, everyone was saying you've got to get serious.
You know, the real money is not in discretionary spending.
And then when they get serious, it's risky, but I think that that's the direction of Republican ideology right now.
And it's a huge political risk, but it's also, in my view, it takes seriously this debt problem.
We're going to see if this is a different political period than the past or not, whether Americans are willing to accept much larger solutions.
We're talking with Michael Gerson.
He's a columnist for the Washington Post and formerly a speechwriter for George W.
Bush.
Of course, the Political Junkie is with us.
You can read his column, download his podcast and solve his ScuttleButton puzzle all at npr.org/junkie.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Ken?
Michael, you say that Paul Ryan's proposal that the $5.3 trillion over 10 years, you can calculate that number, any number of ways...
Right.
...around five trillion.
Okay.
That's fine.
It's serious, but at the same time, he does not touch revenue at all.
He does - like if you talk about what Simpson-Bowles did, that commission, they talked about, yes, cutting spending but also bringing revenue, raising taxes.
They -or any - but he won't touch the military.
He won't touch the wealthy.
So how...
And it may be bold, but he doesn't touch Social Security either.
Right.
So how serious is that?
Well, it's serious where it speaks, let me put it that way.
You know, I - there are, by the way, some members of the Senate, Republican and Democrat, that that want to embody the Simpson-Bowles approach.
Actually legislation...
We had two members of the Gang of Six on last week.
Right, in order to raise these kinds of issues.
I think there's a calculation to some extent that this is what Republicans bring to the table.
It really would be a bridge too far to talk about major tax increases.
And I've known, actually, Paul Ryan for a long time.
He's kind of a growth-oriented Republican.
He's not an austerity guy, even though he has a pretty, you know, austerity-oriented budget.
He really believes economic growth is going to be necessary to maintain in order to meet these goals and these figures.
And he is convinced that tax policy is important to future economic growth.
So, I mean, that's the argument.
If you were to get a grand deal on these issues, it might have other elements.
Do you think if John Boehner ends up taking something closer to the Obama number than to his number of 40 billion that, well, the Democrats say he keeps moving the goalpost.

But anyway, if he ends up taking something closer to the Obama number, his original number, than to the number that the Tea Party asks that he might face a rebellion from within his own party, even a challenge to his position as speaker?
Well, I would say that there are at least three moving pieces to that.
There's the top-line number, which is a matter of debate.
There's the composition of the cuts, which they're arguing about how much of it is kind of mandatory spending and how much of it is...
One time off.
Right.
Exactly.
And then, there's also the very controversial policy riders that have to do with abortion and other issues, planned parenthood, NPR, a variety of these issues.
So there's a whole lot of moving parts in all that about whether he could get an agreement on that.
If he were to essentially give up on all three of those, I think he would have a serious revolt.

If he can make the case, well, I've gotten a better deal than was initially on the table, now, let's focus on the 2012 budget, he might bring along some freshmen there and divide the people that are dividing from the party.
That's the goal.
You're not going to get them all, but can you divide that Tea Party vote.
And we're talking about really - we're talking - what we're talking about is the White House offer of 33 billion and the Tea Party demand of 40 billion.
I mean, we're not talking about a major thing that could result in the shutdown of the government.
Or, indeed, reduce the deficit by a whole lot.
That too.
Well, that's true.
I mean, it's - in the grand scheme of things, it's not a lot of money.
It's symbolic.
You know, who's going to give in, who's going to blink is really the, you know, the question here who gets bragging rights.
But there are some Tea Party people that would have preferred it to be much more than 40 billion and, you know, push for 60.
And, you know, we heard on the tape a hundred, you know, billion in these contexts.
And there, I think Boehner has been very forthright, saying, I mean, I just control the House here.
(Soundbite of laughter)
You know, there are other members of this government.
And we don't even know if he controls it.
Right.
That's the question.
